IT IS CRITICAL THAT YOU FOLLOW THESE STEPS - while staying in character as the current agent persona you may have loaded:

<steps CRITICAL="TRUE">
1. Always LOAD the FULL {project-root}/_bmad/core/tasks/workflow.xml
2. READ its entire contents - this is the CORE OS for EXECUTING the specific workflow-config {project-root}/_bmad/bmm/workflows/4-implementation/create-story/workflow.yaml
3. Pass the yaml path {project-root}/_bmad/bmm/workflows/4-implementation/create-story/workflow.yaml as 'workflow-config' parameter to the workflow.xml instructions
4. Follow workflow.xml instructions EXACTLY as written to process and follow the specific workflow config and its instructions
5. Save outputs after EACH section when generating any documents from templates
</steps>


ARGUMENTS: story_key=5-1-deploy-lgtm-observability-stack

file.content:
<task id="_bmad/core/tasks/workflow.xml" name="Execute Workflow" internal="true">
  <objective>Execute given workflow by loading its configuration, following instructions, and producing output</objective>

  <llm critical="true">
    <mandate>Always read COMPLETE files - NEVER use offset/limit when reading any workflow related files</mandate>
    <mandate>Instructions are MANDATORY - either as file path, steps or embedded list in YAML, XML or markdown</mandate>
    <mandate>Execute ALL steps in instructions IN EXACT ORDER</mandate>
    <mandate>Save to template output file after EVERY "template-output" tag</mandate>
    <mandate>NEVER skip a step - YOU are responsible for every steps execution without fail or excuse</mandate>
  </llm>

  <WORKFLOW-RULES critical="true">
    <rule n="1">Steps execute in exact numerical order (1, 2, 3...)</rule>
    <rule n="2">Optional steps: Ask user unless #yolo mode active</rule>
    <rule n="3">Template-output tags: Save content, discuss with the user the section completed, and NEVER proceed until the users indicates
      to proceed (unless YOLO mode has been activated)</rule>
  </WORKFLOW-RULES>

  <flow>
    <step n="1" title="Load and Initialize Workflow">
      <substep n="1a" title="Load Configuration and Resolve Variables">
        <action>Read workflow.yaml from provided path</action>
        <mandate>Load config_source (REQUIRED for all modules)</mandate>
        <phase n="1">Load external config from config_source path</phase>
        <phase n="2">Resolve all {config_source}: references with values from config</phase>
        <phase n="3">Resolve system variables (date:system-generated) and paths ({project-root}, {installed_path})</phase>
        <phase n="4">Ask user for input of any variables that are still unknown</phase>
      </substep>

      <substep n="1b" title="Load Required Components">
        <mandate>Instructions: Read COMPLETE file from path OR embedded list (REQUIRED)</mandate>
        <check>If template path → Read COMPLETE template file</check>
        <check>If validation path → Note path for later loading when needed</check>
        <check>If template: false → Mark as action-workflow (else template-workflow)</check>
        <note>Data files (csv, json) → Store paths only, load on-demand when instructions reference them</note>
      </substep>

      <substep n="1c" title="Initialize Output" if="template-workflow">
        <action>Resolve default_output_file path with all variables and {{date}}</action>
        <action>Create output directory if doesn't exist</action>
        <action>If template-workflow → Write template to output file with placeholders</action>
        <action>If action-workflow → Skip file creation</action>
      </substep>
    </step>

    <step n="2" title="Process Each Instruction Step in Order">
      <iterate>For each step in instructions:</iterate>

      <substep n="2a" title="Handle Step Attributes">
        <check>If optional="true" and NOT #yolo → Ask user to include</check>
        <check>If if="condition" → Evaluate condition</check>
        <check>If for-each="item" → Repeat step for each item</check>
        <check>If repeat="n" → Repeat step n times</check>
      </substep>

      <substep n="2b" title="Execute Step Content">
        <action>Process step instructions (markdown or XML tags)</action>
        <action>Replace {{variables}} with values (ask user if unknown)</action>
        <execute-tags>
          <tag>action xml tag → Perform the action</tag>
          <tag>check if="condition" xml tag → Conditional block wrapping actions (requires closing </check>)</tag>
          <tag>ask xml tag → Prompt user and WAIT for response</tag>
          <tag>invoke-workflow xml tag → Execute another workflow with given inputs and the workflow.xml runner</tag>
          <tag>invoke-task xml tag → Execute specified task</tag>
          <tag>invoke-protocol name="protocol_name" xml tag → Execute reusable protocol from protocols section</tag>
          <tag>goto step="x" → Jump to specified step</tag>
        </execute-tags>
      </substep>

      <substep n="2c" title="Handle template-output Tags">
        <if tag="template-output">
          <mandate>Generate content for this section</mandate>
          <mandate>Save to file (Write first time, Edit subsequent)</mandate>
          <action>Display generated content</action>
          <ask> [a] Advanced Elicitation, [c] Continue, [p] Party-Mode, [y] YOLO the rest of this document only. WAIT for response. <if
              response="a">
              <action>Start the advanced elicitation workflow {project-root}/_bmad/core/workflows/advanced-elicitation/workflow.xml</action>
            </if>
            <if
              response="c">
              <action>Continue to next step</action>
            </if>
            <if response="p">
              <action>Start the party-mode workflow {project-root}/_bmad/core/workflows/party-mode/workflow.md</action>
            </if>
            <if
              response="y">
              <action>Enter #yolo mode for the rest of the workflow</action>
            </if>
          </ask>
        </if>
      </substep>

      <substep n="2d" title="Step Completion">
        <check>If no special tags and NOT #yolo:</check>
        <ask>Continue to next step? (y/n/edit)</ask>
      </substep>
    </step>

    <step n="3" title="Completion">
      <check>Confirm document saved to output path</check>
      <action>Report workflow completion</action>
    </step>
  </flow>

  <execution-modes>
    <mode name="normal">Full user interaction and confirmation of EVERY step at EVERY template output - NO EXCEPTIONS except yolo MODE</mode>
    <mode name="yolo">Skip all confirmations and elicitation, minimize prompts and try to produce all of the workflow automatically by
      simulating the remaining discussions with an simulated expert user</mode>
  </execution-modes>

  <supported-tags desc="Instructions can use these tags">
    <structural>
      <tag>step n="X" goal="..." - Define step with number and goal</tag>
      <tag>optional="true" - Step can be skipped</tag>
      <tag>if="condition" - Conditional execution</tag>
      <tag>for-each="collection" - Iterate over items</tag>
      <tag>repeat="n" - Repeat n times</tag>
    </structural>
    <execution>
      <tag>action - Required action to perform</tag>
      <tag>action if="condition" - Single conditional action (inline, no closing tag needed)</tag>
      <tag>check if="condition">...</check> - Conditional block wrapping multiple items (closing tag required)</tag>
      <tag>ask - Get user input (ALWAYS wait for response before continuing)</tag>
      <tag>goto - Jump to another step</tag>
      <tag>invoke-workflow - Call another workflow</tag>
      <tag>invoke-task - Call a task</tag>
      <tag>invoke-protocol - Execute a reusable protocol (e.g., discover_inputs)</tag>
    </execution>
    <output>
      <tag>template-output - Save content checkpoint</tag>
      <tag>critical - Cannot be skipped</tag>
      <tag>example - Show example output</tag>
    </output>
  </supported-tags>

  <protocols desc="Reusable workflow protocols that can be invoked via invoke-protocol tag">
    <protocol name="discover_inputs" desc="Smart file discovery and loading based on input_file_patterns">
      <objective>Intelligently load project files (whole or sharded) based on workflow's input_file_patterns configuration</objective>

      <critical>Only execute if workflow.yaml contains input_file_patterns section</critical>

      <flow>
        <step n="1" title="Parse Input File Patterns">
          <action>Read input_file_patterns from loaded workflow.yaml</action>
          <action>For each pattern group (prd, architecture, epics, etc.), note the load_strategy if present</action>
        </step>

        <step n="2" title="Load Files Using Smart Strategies">
          <iterate>For each pattern in input_file_patterns:</iterate>

          <substep n="2a" title="Try Sharded Documents First">
            <check if="sharded pattern exists">
              <action>Determine load_strategy from pattern config (defaults to FULL_LOAD if not specified)</action>

              <strategy name="FULL_LOAD">
                <desc>Load ALL files in sharded directory - used for PRD, Architecture, UX, brownfield docs</desc>
                <action>Use glob pattern to find ALL .md files (e.g., "{output_folder}/*architecture*/*.md")</action>
                <action>Load EVERY matching file completely</action>
                <action>Concatenate content in logical order (index.md first if exists, then alphabetical)</action>
                <action>Store in variable: {pattern_name_content}</action>
              </strategy>

              <strategy name="SELECTIVE_LOAD">
                <desc>Load specific shard using template variable - example: used for epics with {{epic_num}}</desc>
                <action>Check for template variables in sharded_single pattern (e.g., {{epic_num}})</action>
                <action>If variable undefined, ask user for value OR infer from context</action>
                <action>Resolve template to specific file path</action>
                <action>Load that specific file</action>
                <action>Store in variable: {pattern_name_content}</action>
              </strategy>

              <strategy name="INDEX_GUIDED">
                <desc>Load index.md, analyze structure and description of each doc in the index, then intelligently load relevant docs</desc>
                <mandate>DO NOT BE LAZY - use best judgment to load documents that might have relevant information, even if only a 5% chance</mandate>
                <action>Load index.md from sharded directory</action>
                <action>Parse table of contents, links, section headers</action>
                <action>Analyze workflow's purpose and objective</action>
                <action>Identify which linked/referenced documents are likely relevant</action>
                <example>If workflow is about authentication and index shows "Auth Overview", "Payment Setup", "Deployment" → Load auth
                  docs, consider deployment docs, skip payment</example>
                <action>Load all identified relevant documents</action>
                <action>Store combined content in variable: {pattern_name_content}</action>
                <note>When in doubt, LOAD IT - context is valuable, being thorough is better than missing critical info</note>
              </strategy>
              <action>Mark pattern as RESOLVED, skip to next pattern</action>
            </check>
          </substep>

          <substep n="2b" title="Try Whole Document if No Sharded Found">
            <check if="no sharded matches found OR no sharded pattern exists">
              <action>Attempt glob match on 'whole' pattern (e.g., "{output_folder}/*prd*.md")</action>
              <check if="matches found">
                <action>Load ALL matching files completely (no offset/limit)</action>
                <action>Store content in variable: {pattern_name_content} (e.g., {prd_content})</action>
                <action>Mark pattern as RESOLVED, skip to next pattern</action>
              </check>
            </check>
          </substep>

          <substep n="2c" title="Handle Not Found">
            <check if="no matches for sharded OR whole">
              <action>Set {pattern_name_content} to empty string</action>
              <action>Note in session: "No {pattern_name} files found" (not an error, just unavailable, offer use change to provide)</action>
            </check>
          </substep>
        </step>

        <step n="3" title="Report Discovery Results">
          <action>List all loaded content variables with file counts</action>
          <example>
            ✓ Loaded {prd_content} from 5 sharded files: prd/index.md, prd/requirements.md, ...
            ✓ Loaded {architecture_content} from 1 file: Architecture.md
            ✓ Loaded {epics_content} from selective load: epics/epic-3.md
            ○ No ux_design files found
          </example>
          <note>This gives workflow transparency into what context is available</note>
        </step>
      </flow>

    </protocol>
  </protocols>

  <llm final="true">
    <critical-rules>
      • This is the complete workflow execution engine
      • You MUST Follow instructions exactly as written
      • The workflow execution engine is governed by: {project-root}/_bmad/core/tasks/workflow.xml
      • You MUST have already loaded and processed: {installed_path}/workflow.yaml

file.content:
name: create-story
description: 'Creates a dedicated story file with all the context the agent will need to implement it later. Use when the user says "create the next story" or "create story [story identifier]"'

# Critical variables from config
config_source: "{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/config.yaml"
user_name: "{config_source}:user_name"
communication_language: "{config_source}:communication_language"
document_output_language: "{config_source}:document_output_language"
user_skill_level: "{config_source}:user_skill_level"
date: system-generated
planning_artifacts: "{config_source}:planning_artifacts"
implementation_artifacts: "{config_source}:implementation_artifacts"

# Workflow components
installed_path: "{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/workflows/4-implementation/create-story"
template: "{installed_path}/template.md"
instructions: "{installed_path}/instructions.xml"
validation: "{installed_path}/checklist.md"

# Variables and inputs
sprint_status: "{implementation_artifacts}/sprint-status.yaml" # Primary source for story tracking
epics_file: "{planning_artifacts}/epics.md" # Enhanced epics+stories with BDD and source hints
prd_file: "{planning_artifacts}/prd.md" # Fallback for requirements (if not in epics file)
architecture_file: "{planning_artifacts}/architecture.md" # Fallback for constraints (if not in epics file)
ux_file: "{planning_artifacts}/*ux*.md" # Fallback for UX requirements (if not in epics file)
story_title: "" # Will be elicited if not derivable
project_context: "**/project-context.md"
default_output_file: "{implementation_artifacts}/{{story_key}}.md"

# Smart input file references - Simplified for enhanced approach
# The epics+stories file should contain everything needed with source hints
input_file_patterns:
  prd:
    description: "PRD (fallback - epics file should have most content)"
    whole: "{planning_artifacts}/*prd*.md"
    sharded: "{planning_artifacts}/*prd*/*.md"
    load_strategy: "SELECTIVE_LOAD" # Only load if needed
  architecture:
    description: "Architecture (fallback - epics file should have relevant sections)"
    whole: "{planning_artifacts}/*architecture*.md"
    sharded: "{planning_artifacts}/*architecture*/*.md"
    load_strategy: "SELECTIVE_LOAD" # Only load if needed
  ux:
    description: "UX design (fallback - epics file should have relevant sections)"
    whole: "{planning_artifacts}/*ux*.md"
    sharded: "{planning_artifacts}/*ux*/*.md"
    load_strategy: "SELECTIVE_LOAD" # Only load if needed
  epics:
    description: "Enhanced epics+stories file with BDD and source hints"
    whole: "{planning_artifacts}/*epic*.md"
    sharded: "{planning_artifacts}/*epic*/*.md"
    load_strategy: "SELECTIVE_LOAD" # Only load needed epic

<workflow>
  <critical>The workflow execution engine is governed by: {project-root}/_bmad/core/tasks/workflow.xml</critical>
  <critical>You MUST have already loaded and processed: {installed_path}/workflow.yaml</critical>
  <critical>Communicate all responses in {communication_language} and generate all documents in {document_output_language}</critical>

  <critical>🔥 CRITICAL MISSION: You are creating the ULTIMATE story context engine that prevents LLM developer mistakes, omissions or
    disasters! 🔥</critical>
  <critical>Your purpose is NOT to copy from epics - it's to create a comprehensive, optimized story file that gives the DEV agent
    EVERYTHING needed for flawless implementation</critical>
  <critical>COMMON LLM MISTAKES TO PREVENT: reinventing wheels, wrong libraries, wrong file locations, breaking regressions, ignoring UX,
    vague implementations, lying about completion, not learning from past work</critical>
  <critical>🚨 EXHAUSTIVE ANALYSIS REQUIRED: You must thoroughly analyze ALL artifacts to extract critical context - do NOT be lazy or skim!
    This is the most important function in the entire development process!</critical>
  <critical>🔬 UTILIZE SUBPROCESSES AND SUBAGENTS: Use research subagents, subprocesses or parallel processing if available to thoroughly
    analyze different artifacts simultaneously and thoroughly</critical>
  <critical>❓ SAVE QUESTIONS: If you think of questions or clarifications during analysis, save them for the end after the complete story is
    written</critical>
  <critical>🎯 ZERO USER INTERVENTION: Process should be fully automated except for initial epic/story selection or missing documents</critical>

  <step n="1" goal="Determine target story">
    <check if="{{story_path}} is provided by user or user provided the epic and story number such as 2-4 or 1.6 or epic 1 story 5">
      <action>Parse user-provided story path: extract epic_num, story_num, story_title from format like "1-2-user-auth"</action>
      <action>Set {{epic_num}}, {{story_num}}, {{story_key}} from user input</action>
      <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
    </check>

    <action>Check if {{sprint_status}} file exists for auto discover</action>
    <check if="sprint status file does NOT exist">
      <output>🚫 No sprint status file found and no story specified</output>
      <output>
        **Required Options:**
        1. Run `sprint-planning` to initialize sprint tracking (recommended)
        2. Provide specific epic-story number to create (e.g., "1-2-user-auth")
        3. Provide path to story documents if sprint status doesn't exist yet
      </output>
      <ask>Choose option [1], provide epic-story number, path to story docs, or [q] to quit:</ask>

      <check if="user chooses 'q'">
        <action>HALT - No work needed</action>
      </check>

      <check if="user chooses '1'">
        <output>Run sprint-planning workflow first to create sprint-status.yaml</output>
        <action>HALT - User needs to run sprint-planning</action>
      </check>

      <check if="user provides epic-story number">
        <action>Parse user input: extract epic_num, story_num, story_title</action>
        <action>Set {{epic_num}}, {{story_num}}, {{story_key}} from user input</action>
        <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
      </check>

      <check if="user provides story docs path">
        <action>Use user-provided path for story documents</action>
        <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
      </check>
    </check>

    <!-- Auto-discover from sprint status only if no user input -->
    <check if="no user input provided">
      <critical>MUST read COMPLETE {sprint_status} file from start to end to preserve order</critical>
      <action>Load the FULL file: {{sprint_status}}</action>
      <action>Read ALL lines from beginning to end - do not skip any content</action>
      <action>Parse the development_status section completely</action>

      <action>Find the FIRST story (by reading in order from top to bottom) where:
        - Key matches pattern: number-number-name (e.g., "1-2-user-auth")
        - NOT an epic key (epic-X) or retrospective (epic-X-retrospective)
        - Status value equals "backlog"
      </action>

      <check if="no backlog story found">
        <output>📋 No backlog stories found in sprint-status.yaml

          All stories are either already created, in progress, or done.

          **Options:**
          1. Run sprint-planning to refresh story tracking
          2. Load PM agent and run correct-course to add more stories
          3. Check if current sprint is complete and run retrospective
        </output>
        <action>HALT</action>
      </check>

      <action>Extract from found story key (e.g., "1-2-user-authentication"):
        - epic_num: first number before dash (e.g., "1")
        - story_num: second number after first dash (e.g., "2")
        - story_title: remainder after second dash (e.g., "user-authentication")
      </action>
      <action>Set {{story_id}} = "{{epic_num}}.{{story_num}}"</action>
      <action>Store story_key for later use (e.g., "1-2-user-authentication")</action>

      <!-- Mark epic as in-progress if this is first story -->
      <action>Check if this is the first story in epic {{epic_num}} by looking for {{epic_num}}-1-* pattern</action>
      <check if="this is first story in epic {{epic_num}}">
        <action>Load {{sprint_status}} and check epic-{{epic_num}} status</action>
        <action>If epic status is "backlog" → update to "in-progress"</action>
        <action>If epic status is "contexted" (legacy status) → update to "in-progress" (backward compatibility)</action>
        <action>If epic status is "in-progress" → no change needed</action>
        <check if="epic status is 'done'">
          <output>🚫 ERROR: Cannot create story in completed epic</output>
          <output>Epic {{epic_num}} is marked as 'done'. All stories are complete.</output>
          <output>If you need to add more work, either:</output>
          <output>1. Manually change epic status back to 'in-progress' in sprint-status.yaml</output>
          <output>2. Create a new epic for additional work</output>
          <action>HALT - Cannot proceed</action>
        </check>
        <check if="epic status is not one of: backlog, contexted, in-progress, done">
          <output>🚫 ERROR: Invalid epic status '{{epic_status}}'</output>
          <output>Epic {{epic_num}} has invalid status. Expected: backlog, in-progress, or done</output>
          <output>Please fix sprint-status.yaml manually or run sprint-planning to regenerate</output>
          <action>HALT - Cannot proceed</action>
        </check>
        <output>📊 Epic {{epic_num}} status updated to in-progress</output>
      </check>

      <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
    </check>
    <action>Load the FULL file: {{sprint_status}}</action>
    <action>Read ALL lines from beginning to end - do not skip any content</action>
    <action>Parse the development_status section completely</action>

    <action>Find the FIRST story (by reading in order from top to bottom) where:
      - Key matches pattern: number-number-name (e.g., "1-2-user-auth")
      - NOT an epic key (epic-X) or retrospective (epic-X-retrospective)
      - Status value equals "backlog"
    </action>

    <check if="no backlog story found">
      <output>📋 No backlog stories found in sprint-status.yaml

        All stories are either already created, in progress, or done.

        **Options:**
        1. Run sprint-planning to refresh story tracking
        2. Load PM agent and run correct-course to add more stories
        3. Check if current sprint is complete and run retrospective
      </output>
      <action>HALT</action>
    </check>

    <action>Extract from found story key (e.g., "1-2-user-authentication"):
      - epic_num: first number before dash (e.g., "1")
      - story_num: second number after first dash (e.g., "2")
      - story_title: remainder after second dash (e.g., "user-authentication")
    </action>
    <action>Set {{story_id}} = "{{epic_num}}.{{story_num}}"</action>
    <action>Store story_key for later use (e.g., "1-2-user-authentication")</action>

    <!-- Mark epic as in-progress if this is first story -->
    <action>Check if this is the first story in epic {{epic_num}} by looking for {{epic_num}}-1-* pattern</action>
    <check if="this is first story in epic {{epic_num}}">
      <action>Load {{sprint_status}} and check epic-{{epic_num}} status</action>
      <action>If epic status is "backlog" → update to "in-progress"</action>
      <action>If epic status is "contexted" (legacy status) → update to "in-progress" (backward compatibility)</action>
      <action>If epic status is "in-progress" → no change needed</action>
      <check if="epic status is 'done'">
        <output>🚫 ERROR: Cannot create story in completed epic</output>
        <output>Epic {{epic_num}} is marked as 'done'. All stories are complete.</output>
        <output>If you need to add more work, either:</output>
        <output>1. Manually change epic status back to 'in-progress' in sprint-status.yaml</output>
        <output>2. Create a new epic for additional work</output>
        <action>HALT - Cannot proceed</action>
      </check>
      <check if="epic status is not one of: backlog, contexted, in-progress, done">
        <output>🚫 ERROR: Invalid epic status '{{epic_status}}'</output>
        <output>Epic {{epic_num}} has invalid status. Expected: backlog, in-progress, or done</output>
        <output>Please fix sprint-status.yaml manually or run sprint-planning to regenerate</output>
        <action>HALT - Cannot proceed</action>
      </check>
      <output>📊 Epic {{epic_num}} status updated to in-progress</output>
    </check>

    <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
  </step>

  <step n="2" goal="Load and analyze core artifacts">
    <critical>🔬 EXHAUSTIVE ARTIFACT ANALYSIS - This is where you prevent future developer fuckups!</critical>

    <!-- Load all available content through discovery protocol -->
    <invoke-protocol
      name="discover_inputs" />
    <note>Available content: {epics_content}, {prd_content}, {architecture_content}, {ux_content},
    {project_context}</note>

    <!-- Analyze epics file for story foundation -->
    <action>From {epics_content}, extract Epic {{epic_num}} complete context:</action> **EPIC ANALYSIS:** - Epic
    objectives and business value - ALL stories in this epic for cross-story context - Our specific story's requirements, user story
    statement, acceptance criteria - Technical requirements and constraints - Dependencies on other stories/epics - Source hints pointing to
    original documents <!-- Extract specific story requirements -->
    <action>Extract our story ({{epic_num}}-{{story_num}}) details:</action> **STORY FOUNDATION:** - User story statement
    (As a, I want, so that) - Detailed acceptance criteria (already BDD formatted) - Technical requirements specific to this story -
    Business context and value - Success criteria <!-- Previous story analysis for context continuity -->
    <check if="story_num > 1">
      <action>Find {{previous_story_num}}: scan {implementation_artifacts} for the story file in epic {{epic_num}} with the highest story number less than {{story_num}}</action>
      <action>Load previous story file: {implementation_artifacts}/{{epic_num}}-{{previous_story_num}}-*.md</action> **PREVIOUS STORY INTELLIGENCE:** -
    Dev notes and learnings from previous story - Review feedback and corrections needed - Files that were created/modified and their
    patterns - Testing approaches that worked/didn't work - Problems encountered and solutions found - Code patterns established <action>Extract
    all learnings that could impact current story implementation</action>
    </check>

    <!-- Git intelligence for previous work patterns -->
    <check
      if="previous story exists AND git repository detected">
      <action>Get last 5 commit titles to understand recent work patterns</action>
      <action>Analyze 1-5 most recent commits for relevance to current story:
        - Files created/modified
        - Code patterns and conventions used
        - Library dependencies added/changed
        - Architecture decisions implemented
        - Testing approaches used
      </action>
      <action>Extract actionable insights for current story implementation</action>
    </check>
  </step>

  <step n="3" goal="Architecture analysis for developer guardrails">
    <critical>🏗️ ARCHITECTURE INTELLIGENCE - Extract everything the developer MUST follow!</critical> **ARCHITECTURE DOCUMENT ANALYSIS:** <action>Systematically
    analyze architecture content for story-relevant requirements:</action>

    <!-- Load architecture - single file or sharded -->
    <check if="architecture file is single file">
      <action>Load complete {architecture_content}</action>
    </check>
    <check if="architecture is sharded to folder">
      <action>Load architecture index and scan all architecture files</action>
    </check> **CRITICAL ARCHITECTURE EXTRACTION:** <action>For
    each architecture section, determine if relevant to this story:</action> - **Technical Stack:** Languages, frameworks, libraries with
    versions - **Code Structure:** Folder organization, naming conventions, file patterns - **API Patterns:** Service structure, endpoint
    patterns, data contracts - **Database Schemas:** Tables, relationships, constraints relevant to story - **Security Requirements:**
    Authentication patterns, authorization rules - **Performance Requirements:** Caching strategies, optimization patterns - **Testing
    Standards:** Testing frameworks, coverage expectations, test patterns - **Deployment Patterns:** Environment configurations, build
    processes - **Integration Patterns:** External service integrations, data flows <action>Extract any story-specific requirements that the
    developer MUST follow</action>
    <action>Identify any architectural decisions that override previous patterns</action>
  </step>

  <step n="4" goal="Web research for latest technical specifics">
    <critical>🌐 ENSURE LATEST TECH KNOWLEDGE - Prevent outdated implementations!</critical> **WEB INTELLIGENCE:** <action>Identify specific
    technical areas that require latest version knowledge:</action>

    <!-- Check for libraries/frameworks mentioned in architecture -->
    <action>From architecture analysis, identify specific libraries, APIs, or
    frameworks</action>
    <action>For each critical technology, research latest stable version and key changes:
      - Latest API documentation and breaking changes
      - Security vulnerabilities or updates
      - Performance improvements or deprecations
      - Best practices for current version
    </action>
    **EXTERNAL CONTEXT INCLUSION:** <action>Include in story any critical latest information the developer needs:
      - Specific library versions and why chosen
      - API endpoints with parameters and authentication
      - Recent security patches or considerations
      - Performance optimization techniques
      - Migration considerations if upgrading
    </action>
  </step>

  <step n="5" goal="Create comprehensive story file">
    <critical>📝 CREATE ULTIMATE STORY FILE - The developer's master implementation guide!</critical>

    <action>Initialize from template.md:
    {default_output_file}</action>
    <template-output file="{default_output_file}">story_header</template-output>

    <!-- Story foundation from epics analysis -->
    <template-output
      file="{default_output_file}">story_requirements</template-output>

    <!-- Developer context section - MOST IMPORTANT PART -->
    <template-output file="{default_output_file}">
    developer_context_section</template-output> **DEV AGENT GUARDRAILS:** <template-output file="{default_output_file}">
    technical_requirements</template-output>
    <template-output file="{default_output_file}">architecture_compliance</template-output>
    <template-output
      file="{default_output_file}">library_framework_requirements</template-output>
    <template-output file="{default_output_file}">
    file_structure_requirements</template-output>
    <template-output file="{default_output_file}">testing_requirements</template-output>

    <!-- Previous story intelligence -->
    <check
      if="previous story learnings available">
      <template-output file="{default_output_file}">previous_story_intelligence</template-output>
    </check>

    <!-- Git intelligence -->
    <check
      if="git analysis completed">
      <template-output file="{default_output_file}">git_intelligence_summary</template-output>
    </check>

    <!-- Latest technical specifics -->
    <check if="web research completed">
      <template-output file="{default_output_file}">latest_tech_information</template-output>
    </check>

    <!-- Project context reference -->
    <template-output
      file="{default_output_file}">project_context_reference</template-output>

    <!-- Final status update -->
    <template-output file="{default_output_file}">
    story_completion_status</template-output>

    <!-- CRITICAL: Set status to ready-for-dev -->
    <action>Set story Status to: "ready-for-dev"</action>
    <action>Add completion note: "Ultimate
    context engine analysis completed - comprehensive developer guide created"</action>
  </step>

  <step n="6" goal="Update sprint status and finalize">
    <invoke-task>Validate against checklist at {installed_path}/checklist.md using _bmad/core/tasks/validate-workflow.xml</invoke-task>
    <action>Save story document unconditionally</action>

    <!-- Update sprint status -->
    <check if="sprint status file exists">
      <action>Update {{sprint_status}}</action>
      <action>Load the FULL file and read all development_status entries</action>
      <action>Find development_status key matching {{story_key}}</action>
      <action>Verify current status is "backlog" (expected previous state)</action>
      <action>Update development_status[{{story_key}}] = "ready-for-dev"</action>
      <action>Save file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS</action>
    </check>

    <action>Report completion</action>
    <output>**🎯 ULTIMATE BMad Method STORY CONTEXT CREATED, {user_name}!**

      **Story Details:**
      - Story ID: {{story_id}}
      - Story Key: {{story_key}}
      - File: {{story_file}}
      - Status: ready-for-dev

      **Next Steps:**
      1. Review the comprehensive story in {{story_file}}
      2. Run dev agents `dev-story` for optimized implementation
      3. Run `code-review` when complete (auto-marks done)
      4. Optional: If Test Architect module installed, run `/bmad:tea:automate` after `dev-story` to generate guardrail tests

      **The developer now has everything needed for flawless implementation!**
    </output>
  </step>

</workflow>

1→---
     2→stepsCompleted:
     3→  - step-01-validate-prerequisites
     4→  - step-02-design-epics
     5→  - step-03-create-stories

     9→  - _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/architecture.md
    10→---
    11→
    12→# src - Epic Breakdown
    13→
    14→## Overview
    15→
    16→This document provides the complete epic and story breakdown for the Python Automation Governance Framework, decomposing the requirements from the PRD and Architecture into implementable stories.
    17→
    18→## Requirements Inventory
    19→
    20→### Functional Requirements
    21→
    22→- **FR1:** Developer can clone a standardized boilerplate template from the repository to start a new script
    23→- **FR2:** Developer can choose between a minimal or full base Docker image for their script
    24→- **FR3:** Developer can place their business logic into the designated `src/` directory without modifying files outside that directory (governance framework code in `framework/` and `config/` directories is read-only to developers)
    25→- **FR4:** Developer can reference Key Vault secrets via environment variables without knowing the underlying credential values
    26→- **FR5:** Developer can define the CronJob schedule (cron expression) for their script in a configuration file
    27→- **FR6:** Developer can document their script in a standardized README containing required sections: Purpose, Schedule, Inputs, Outputs, Owner, and Dependencies
    28→- **FR7:** Developer can push code to Azure DevOps and create a Pull Request for review
    29→- **FR8:** CI pipeline can automatically validate the submitted code (build, security scan, template compliance)
    30→- **FR9:** Reviewer can see CI pipeline results alongside the Pull Request
    31→- **FR10:** Reviewer can validate code against the security checklist: (1) no hardcoded credentials, (2) template structure compliance, (3) structured logging present, (4) error handling with non-zero exit codes, (5) README completeness
    32→- **FR11:** Reviewer can comment on the Pull Request requesting changes
    33→- **FR12:** Reviewer can approve and merge the Pull Request to trigger deployment
    34→- **FR13:** System can reject PRs that contain detected credentials or secrets in code
    35→- **FR14:** System can automatically build a Docker image and push it to Azure Container Registry upon PR merge
    36→- **FR15:** ArgoCD can detect new or updated images and sync deployments to the AKS cluster
    37→- **FR16:** System can execute Python scripts as Kubernetes CronJobs on the defined schedule
    38→- **FR17:** System can execute Python scripts as on-demand Kubernetes Jobs triggered via CLI command (kubectl or ArgoCD)
    39→- **FR18:** System can automatically retry failed jobs with increasing delay between retries (up to 3 retries)
    40→- **FR19:** System can enforce timeout limits per job execution (default 30 minutes, configurable per CronJob up to 4 hours)
    41→- **FR20:** System can enforce resource quotas (CPU, memory) per namespace and per job
    42→- **FR21:** Admin can provision service account credentials in Azure Key Vault per business area
    43→- **FR22:** System can inject Key Vault secrets into CronJob pods at runtime
    44→- **FR23:** System can scope credential access per namespace (logistics scripts cannot access finance secrets)
    45→- **FR24:** System can audit all credential access events — logged to Key Vault audit logs with caller identity, timestamp, and operation type, retained for minimum 90 days
    46→- **FR25:** Operator can view a platform-wide dashboard showing all namespaces, total jobs, and aggregate success rates
    47→- **FR26:** Operator can drill down into a specific namespace to see all CronJobs and their status
    48→- **FR27:** Operator can view individual CronJob execution history (minimum last 100 runs or 90 days, whichever is greater, with duration and status per run)
    49→- **FR28:** Operator can view logs for any specific job execution
    50→- **FR29:** Operator can view duration trends for each CronJob over time
    51→- **FR30:** System can collect and display per-CronJob resource consumption metrics (CPU, memory, duration)
    52→- **FR31:** System can track compute cost (CPU-seconds, memory-seconds) per individual CronJob execution
    53→- **FR32:** System can aggregate costs per namespace (daily, weekly, monthly)
    54→- **FR33:** Operator can view a cost breakdown dashboard per CronJob and per namespace
    55→- **FR34:** Operator can export monthly cost reports per namespace for chargeback in CSV format via Grafana dashboard export
    56→- **FR35:** System can send alerts to Microsoft Teams when a job fails after all retries
    57→- **FR36:** System can send alerts when job execution time exceeds 2x the historical average
    58→- **FR37:** System can route alerts by severity level (Critical → Teams + Email, High → Teams + Email, Medium → Email, Low → Dashboard only)
    59→- **FR38:** Operator can view active alerts and alert history in the dashboard
    60→- **FR39:** Admin can create and configure namespaces per business area
    61→- **FR40:** Admin can map AD security groups to namespace-scoped RBAC roles
    62→- **FR41:** Developer can only view and interact with resources in their own namespace
    63→- **FR42:** Reviewer can view resources across all namespaces (read-only)
    64→- **FR43:** Admin can set and adjust resource quotas per namespace
    65→- **FR44:** Admin can provision AKS clusters for DEV and PRD environments
    66→- **FR45:** Admin can configure ArgoCD to watch specific repository folders per namespace
    67→- **FR46:** System can collect logs centrally via Loki for all job executions across namespaces
    68→- **FR47:** Admin can configure base Docker images and publish them to the Container Registry
    69→
    70→### NonFunctional Requirements
    71→
    72→- **NFR1:** Zero secrets in code, config files, or environment variable definitions — CI secret scanning (e.g., TruffleHog or CredScan) blocks PRs containing detected credentials, verified on every pull request
    73→- **NFR2:** Key Vault access scoped per namespace — automated test verifies logistics pods cannot retrieve finance secrets, executed on every infrastructure change and weekly scheduled scan
    74→- **NFR3:** 100% of credential access events recorded in Key Vault audit logs — completeness verified monthly by comparing access count against application request count, with < 1% discrepancy tolerance
    75→- **NFR4:** Container images scanned for known vulnerabilities (CVEs) during CI pipeline — zero Critical/High CVEs allowed before push to ACR, verified per build
    76→- **NFR5:** RBAC permissions verified by automated test suite — each namespace role can only access its designated resources, tests run on every infrastructure change and weekly scheduled scan
    77→- **NFR6:** CronJob pods cannot communicate with pods in other namespaces — cross-namespace network isolation verified by automated connectivity tests on every infrastructure change and weekly scheduled scan
    78→- **NFR7:** All API and dashboard access requires corporate AD (SSO) authentication — zero anonymous access paths, verified by automated access audit on every infrastructure change and quarterly penetration test
    79→- **NFR8:** Production CronJob execution availability ≥ 99.5% measured monthly (critical batch jobs: SAP invoice processing, month-end reconciliation)
    80→- **NFR9:** Failed jobs retry automatically up to 3 times with increasing delay between retries — alert sent to Teams within 2 minutes of final retry failure, measured via Grafana alert rule evaluation
    81→- **NFR10:** Mean failure detection time < 5 minutes via Grafana alerting to Teams + Email
    82→- **NFR11:** No single point of failure — cluster maintains workload availability during any single node failure, verified by quarterly chaos test (node drain) with zero missed scheduled CronJob executions
    83→- **NFR12:** ArgoCD self-heals drift — if a CronJob manifest is manually modified in-cluster, ArgoCD reverts to Git-defined state within 3 minutes
    84→- **NFR13:** Key Vault secret rotation does not cause job failures — running jobs continue using valid credentials through rotation events, verified by monthly rotation test with zero job failures during 24-hour observation window
    85→- **NFR14:** Platform supports scaling from pilot (5-10 CronJobs) to full rollout (60+ CronJobs) — verified by load test deploying 60 concurrent CronJobs with all jobs completing within defined timeout and zero resource contention errors
    86→- **NFR15:** AKS cluster supports horizontal node autoscaling (3-10 nodes) based on pending pod demand
    87→- **NFR16:** Each namespace supports up to 20 concurrent CronJobs without resource contention
    88→- **NFR17:** New business area onboarding (namespace + RBAC + Key Vault) completable within 2 hours of manual admin work
    89→- **NFR18:** CronJob pod startup time (image pull + container init) < 60 seconds for cached images, < 120 seconds for cold pull
    90→- **NFR19:** Grafana dashboard page load < 3 seconds for up to 30 days of execution history
    91→- **NFR20:** CI pipeline (build + security scan + push to ACR) completes in < 10 minutes for standard scripts
    92→- **NFR21:** Log query response time (Loki) < 5 seconds for last 7 days of logs per CronJob
    93→- **NFR22:** SAP connectivity from AKS pods verified end-to-end — network path, name resolution, and authentication functional before pilot launch, measured by automated smoke test completing a read operation against SAP within 10 seconds, executed daily
    94→- **NFR23:** ArgoCD sync latency < 5 minutes from merge-to-main to CronJob updated in cluster
    95→- **NFR24:** Grafana alerting delivers Teams notifications within 60 seconds of alert condition trigger
    96→- **NFR25:** Azure DevOps CI pipeline triggers automatically on PR creation and on merge to main — trigger reliability ≥ 99% measured monthly (zero missed triggers per 100 events), verified via pipeline run history audit
    97→- **NFR26:** Prometheus metrics scraping interval ≤ 30 seconds for CronJob execution metrics
    98→
    99→### Additional Requirements
   100→
   101→**From Architecture — Infrastructure & Platform Decisions:**
   102→
   103→- Monorepo structure: `terraform/` for IaC modules, `k8s/` for Kubernetes manifests
   104→- Terraform state backend on Azure Storage Account with RA-GRS replication and per-module state file segmentation (7 state files: networking, aks, security, monitoring, argocd, namespaces, ci)
   105→- VNet peering from AKS VNet to SAP private network + Private DNS zones for SAP hostname resolution
   106→- Default-deny NetworkPolicy per namespace with explicit egress allowlists (Calico or Azure NPM)
   107→- Trivy Operator for continuous in-cluster vulnerability scanning (complements CI-time Trivy scan)
   108→- ArgoCD ApplicationSets with Git generator — auto-discovers new namespace directories in `k8s/apps/*/`
   109→- Image tagging: Git SHA (immutable) + `latest-<env>` mutable tags for ArgoCD Image Updater
   110→- Manual approval gate for DEV → PRD promotion via Azure DevOps Release Pipeline
   111→- OTel Operator auto-instrumentation for Python — zero-code traces sent to Tempo via annotations
   112→- Structured JSON logs via `structlog` with standard fields: timestamp, level, message, cronjob_name, namespace, trace_id, run_id
   113→- Robusta for alert enrichment (pod logs, resource usage, suggested fixes) → Teams/Email sinks
   114→- Azure Storage Account with 3 containers for LGTM backends: `mimir-blocks`, `loki-chunks`, `tempo-traces`
   115→- Workload Identity (User-Assigned Managed Identity per namespace) for zero-trust pod authentication
   116→- Two-tier base Docker images: `python-gov-base:minimal` (~150MB) and `python-gov-base:full` (~500MB)
   117→- Key Vault CSI Driver with SecretProviderClass per namespace, rotationPollInterval: 2m
   118→- Single Grafana instance embedded in Robusta's kube-prometheus-stack connects to Mimir, Loki, and Tempo
   119→
   120→**From Architecture — Implementation Sequence (dependency order):**
   121→
   122→1. Networking (VNet, subnets, peering, DNS)
   123→2. AKS cluster (requires networking)
   124→3. Security (Key Vault, CSI driver, managed identities — requires AKS)
   125→4. Monitoring (LGTM + Robusta + OTel Operator — requires AKS + storage)
   126→5. ArgoCD (requires AKS + networking)
   127→6. Namespaces (RBAC, quotas, network policies, SecretProviderClass — requires all above)
   128→7. CI (ACR, pipelines, base images — requires AKS + ACR)
   129→8. Boilerplate template (requires all platform services running)
   130→
   131→### FR Coverage Map
   132→
   133→- FR1: Epic 2 — Clone boilerplate template
   134→- FR2: Epic 2 — Choose base Docker image
   135→- FR3: Epic 2 — Business logic in src/ directory
   136→- FR4: Epic 3 — Key Vault secret references via env vars
   137→- FR5: Epic 2 — CronJob schedule configuration
   138→- FR6: Epic 2 — Standardized README documentation
   139→- FR7: Epic 4 — Push code and create PR
   140→- FR8: Epic 4 — CI pipeline validation (build, scan, compliance)
   141→- FR9: Epic 4 — CI results visible in PR
   142→- FR10: Epic 4 — Security checklist validation
   143→- FR11: Epic 4 — PR commenting for changes
   144→- FR12: Epic 4 — PR approval triggers deployment
   145→- FR13: Epic 4 — Reject PRs with detected credentials
   146→- FR14: Epic 4 — Auto-build Docker image and push to ACR
   147→- FR15: Epic 4 — ArgoCD sync deployments
   148→- FR16: Epic 2 — CronJob scheduled execution
   149→- FR17: Epic 2 — On-demand job execution via CLI
   150→- FR18: Epic 2 — Auto-retry with increasing delay
   151→- FR19: Epic 2 — Timeout enforcement per job
   152→- FR20: Epic 1 — Resource quotas per namespace and job
   153→- FR21: Epic 3 — Key Vault credential provisioning
   154→- FR22: Epic 3 — Secret injection into pods at runtime
   155→- FR23: Epic 3 — Credential scoping per namespace
   156→- FR24: Epic 3 — Credential access audit logging
   157→- FR25: Epic 5 — Platform-wide dashboard
   158→- FR26: Epic 5 — Namespace drill-down view
   159→- FR27: Epic 5 — CronJob execution history
   160→- FR28: Epic 5 — Job execution log viewer
   161→- FR29: Epic 5 — Duration trends visualization
   162→- FR30: Epic 5 — Per-CronJob resource metrics
   163→- FR31: Epic 6 — Per-CronJob cost tracking
   164→- FR32: Epic 6 — Namespace cost aggregation
   165→- FR33: Epic 6 — Cost breakdown dashboard
   166→- FR34: Epic 6 — Monthly CSV export for chargeback
   167→- FR35: Epic 7 — Teams alert on job failure
   168→- FR36: Epic 7 — Anomaly alert (2x average duration)
   169→- FR37: Epic 7 — Severity-based alert routing
   170→- FR38: Epic 7 — Alert dashboard and history
   171→- FR39: Epic 1 — Namespace creation and configuration
   172→- FR40: Epic 1 — AD group to RBAC role mapping
   173→- FR41: Epic 1 — Developer namespace isolation
   174→- FR42: Epic 1 — Reviewer cross-namespace read access
   175→- FR43: Epic 1 — Resource quota management
   176→- FR44: Epic 1 — AKS cluster provisioning (DEV + PRD)
   177→- FR45: Epic 4 — ArgoCD per-namespace folder watching
   178→- FR46: Epic 5 — Loki centralized log collection
   179→- FR47: Epic 2 — Base Docker image management
   180→
   181→**Coverage: 47/47 FRs — Zero gaps**
   182→
   183→## Epic List
   184→
   185→1. **Platform Infrastructure** — Juliano can provision a governed AKS platform with networking, namespace isolation, RBAC, resource quotas, and Azure Container Registry — the foundation all other epics build upon.
   186→   FRs: FR20, FR39, FR40, FR41, FR42, FR43, FR44 | NFRs: NFR5, NFR6, NFR7, NFR11, NFR14, NFR15, NFR16, NFR17, NFR22
   187→
   188→2. **Developer Onboarding & Script Execution** — Marcos can onboard his Python script using the boilerplate template and run it reliably as a scheduled CronJob with retries, timeouts, and resource limits.
   189→   FRs: FR1, FR2, FR3, FR5, FR6, FR16, FR17, FR18, FR19, FR47 | NFRs: NFR8, NFR18
   190→
   191→3. **Secure Credential Management** — Marcos can securely reference SAP and Azure credentials without knowing secret values. Juliano provisions and scopes secrets per business area with full audit trail.
   192→   FRs: FR4, FR21, FR22, FR23, FR24 | NFRs: NFR1, NFR2, NFR3, NFR6, NFR13
   193→
   194→4. **Automated CI/CD & Code Governance** — Marcos submits code via PR, CI validates automatically, Christian reviews and approves, and ArgoCD deploys via GitOps — the complete governance pipeline.
   195→   FRs: FR7, FR8, FR9, FR10, FR11, FR12, FR13, FR14, FR15, FR45 | NFRs: NFR1, NFR4, NFR12, NFR20, NFR23, NFR25
   196→
   197→5. **Monitoring, Logging & Observability** — Ana can see execution status and logs from the dashboard without asking IT. Juliano has full platform-wide visibility into all namespaces, execution history, and resource consumption.
   198→   FRs: FR25, FR26, FR27, FR28, FR29, FR30, FR46 | NFRs: NFR19, NFR21, NFR22, NFR26
   199→
   200→6. **Cost Tracking & Chargeback** — Ana and Juliano can track compute costs per script and per business area, and export monthly chargeback reports for finance.
   201→   FRs: FR31, FR32, FR33, FR34
   202→
   203→7. **Alerting & Incident Response** — Ana and Juliano receive proactive notifications via Teams and Email when jobs fail or behave anomalously, with severity-based routing and full alert history.
   204→   FRs: FR35, FR36, FR37, FR38 | NFRs: NFR9, NFR10, NFR24
   205→
   206→---
   207→
   208→## Epic 1: Platform Infrastructure
   209→
   210→Juliano can provision a governed AKS platform with networking, namespace isolation, RBAC, resource quotas, and Azure Container Registry — the foundation all other epics build upon.
   211→
   212→### Story 1.1: Provision Network Infrastructure
   213→
   214→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   215→I want VNet, subnets, VNet peering to SAP, and Private DNS zones provisioned via Terraform,
   216→So that AKS can securely connect to SAP and Azure services.
   217→
   218→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   219→
   220→**Given** Terraform networking module is applied
   221→**When** the infrastructure is provisioned
   222→**Then** VNet with required subnets exists in the target resource group
   223→**And** VNet peering to SAP private network is established and connected
   224→**And** Private DNS zones resolve SAP hostnames from within the VNet
   225→**And** SAP connectivity smoke test completes a read operation within 10 seconds from within the VNet (NFR22)
   226→**And** Terraform state is stored in `networking/terraform.tfstate`
   227→
   228→### Story 1.2: Provision AKS Clusters for DEV and PRD
   229→
   230→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   231→I want AKS clusters provisioned in DEV and PRD environments with autoscaling node pools,
   232→So that the platform has a reliable execution environment for Python CronJobs.
   233→
   234→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   235→
   236→**Given** networking infrastructure from Story 1.1 is provisioned
   237→**When** Terraform AKS module is applied for each environment
   238→**Then** AKS cluster is operational in DEV environment
   239→**And** AKS cluster is operational in PRD environment
   240→**And** Node pools support autoscaling from 3 to 10 nodes
   241→**And** Cluster maintains workload availability during single node failure with zero missed CronJob executions (NFR11)
   242→**And** Azure Container Registry (ACR) is provisioned and accessible from both AKS clusters
   243→**And** Terraform state is stored in `aks/terraform.tfstate`
   244→
   245→### Story 1.3: Configure Namespace Isolation with RBAC and Quotas
   246→
   247→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   248→I want namespaces with AD-group-based RBAC, resource quotas, and default-deny network policies,
   249→So that business areas are isolated and resource consumption is controlled.
   250→
   251→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   252→
   253→**Given** AKS cluster is operational
   254→**When** Terraform namespace module is applied for `logistica` namespace
   255→**Then** namespace exists with resource quotas (CPU: 2 req/4 limit cores, Memory: 2/4 Gi)
   256→**And** GRP-PYTHON-DEV AD group is mapped to developer role (view pods, logs in own namespace only)
   257→**And** GRP-PYTHON-REVIEW AD group has read-only access across all namespaces
   258→**And** GRP-PYTHON-OPS AD group can view pods, logs, exec across all areas
   259→**And** GRP-PYTHON-ADMIN AD group has full cluster admin access
   260→**And** default-deny NetworkPolicy blocks cross-namespace pod communication
   261→**And** explicit egress allows DNS, SAP endpoints, and Azure services only
   262→
   263→---
   264→
   265→## Epic 2: Developer Onboarding & Script Execution
   266→
   267→Marcos can onboard his Python script using the boilerplate template and run it reliably as a scheduled CronJob with retries, timeouts, and resource limits.
   268→
   269→### Story 2.1: Build and Publish Base Docker Images
   270→
   271→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   272→I want standardized minimal and full base Docker images published to Azure Container Registry,
   273→So that developers have secure, consistent Python runtimes to build upon.
   274→
   275→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   276→
   277→**Given** ACR is provisioned
   278→**When** base images are built and pushed
   279→**Then** `python-gov-base:minimal` exists in ACR with Python 3.13, logging framework, Key Vault SDK, Prometheus client (~150MB)
   280→**And** `python-gov-base:full` exists in ACR with pandas, numpy, openpyxl, requests, httpx, SAP libraries, sqlalchemy (~500MB)
   281→**And** both images pass Trivy vulnerability scan with zero Critical/High CVEs
   282→**And** images are tagged with build date and `latest`
   283→
   284→### Story 2.2: Create CronJob Boilerplate Template
   285→
   286→As a Business Developer (Marcos),
   287→I want a boilerplate template with project structure, structured logging, schedule config, and README template,
   288→So that I can onboard my Python script to the platform with minimal friction.
   289→
   290→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   291→
   292→**Given** the boilerplate repository exists in Azure DevOps
   293→**When** a developer clones the template
   294→**Then** `src/` directory exists for business logic with `executar()` entry point
   295→**And** `framework/` and `config/` directories contain read-only governance code
   296→**And** Dockerfile references a base image choice (minimal or full)
   297→**And** `config.yaml` includes a cron schedule expression field
   298→**And** `structlog` is pre-configured with standard fields (timestamp, level, message, cronjob_name, namespace, trace_id, run_id)
   299→**And** README template includes required sections: Purpose, Schedule, Inputs, Outputs, Owner, Dependencies
   300→**And** developer can run the template locally for testing
   301→
   302→### Story 2.3: Deploy and Execute Scheduled CronJobs
   303→
   304→As a Business Developer (Marcos),
   305→I want my script to run as a Kubernetes CronJob on the defined schedule with retries and timeouts,
   306→So that it executes reliably without my intervention.
   307→
   308→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   309→
   310→**Given** a script is deployed to a namespace as a CronJob
   311→**When** the cron schedule triggers
   312→**Then** the CronJob executes on the defined schedule
   313→**And** failed jobs retry automatically up to 3 times with increasing delay
   314→**And** jobs enforce a timeout limit (default 30 minutes, configurable up to 4 hours)
   315→**And** per-job resource limits are enforced (CPU: 250m req/1 core limit, Memory: 256Mi req/1Gi limit)
   316→**And** pod startup time is < 60 seconds for cached images
   317→**And** production CronJob execution availability is >= 99.5% measured monthly (NFR8)
   318→
   319→### Story 2.4: Execute On-Demand Jobs via CLI
   320→
   321→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   322→I want to trigger job execution on-demand via kubectl or ArgoCD,
   323→So that I can test scripts or run ad-hoc executions outside the schedule.
   324→
   325→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   326→
   327→**Given** a CronJob is deployed in a namespace
   328→**When** an admin runs `kubectl create job --from=cronjob/<name>` or triggers via ArgoCD
   329→**Then** the job executes immediately with the same configuration as the scheduled version
   330→**And** execution results are visible in monitoring dashboards
   331→**And** the on-demand job respects the same resource limits and timeout as the scheduled version
   332→
   333→---
   334→
   335→## Epic 3: Secure Credential Management
   336→
   337→Marcos can securely reference SAP and Azure credentials without knowing secret values. Juliano provisions and scopes secrets per business area with full audit trail.
   338→
   339→### Story 3.1: Provision Key Vault with Namespace-Scoped Access
   340→
   341→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   342→I want Azure Key Vault configured with CSI driver and per-namespace managed identities,
   343→So that secrets are isolated per business area and pods authenticate via workload identity.
   344→
   345→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   346→
   347→**Given** AKS cluster and namespaces are provisioned
   348→**When** Terraform security module is applied
   349→**Then** Key Vault `kv-hypera-dev` and `kv-hypera-prd` exist
   350→**And** User-Assigned Managed Identity is created per namespace
   351→**And** CSI Secret Store driver is installed on the cluster
   352→**And** SecretProviderClass resource is deployed per namespace, scoped to that namespace's secrets only
   353→**And** rotationPollInterval is set to 2 minutes
   354→**And** logistics namespace pods cannot retrieve finance namespace secrets (verified by test)
   355→
   356→### Story 3.2: Inject Secrets into CronJob Pods at Runtime
   357→
   358→As a Business Developer (Marcos),
   359→I want to reference secrets via environment variables that are injected automatically at runtime,
   360→So that I never handle raw credential values in my code.
   361→
   362→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   363→
   364→**Given** Key Vault contains a secret for a namespace
   365→**When** a CronJob pod starts in that namespace
   366→**Then** the secret is mounted via CSI driver as a volume
   367→**And** the secret value is available as an environment variable in the pod
   368→**And** the developer's code references the secret via `os.environ["SECRET_NAME"]`
   369→**And** no secret values appear in Kubernetes Secret objects in etcd
   370→**And** the boilerplate template documents how to reference secrets
   371→
   372→### Story 3.3: Enable Credential Access Auditing
   373→
   374→As a Security Officer,
   375→I want all credential access events logged with caller identity, timestamp, and operation type,
   376→So that we have a complete audit trail retained for minimum 90 days.
   377→
   378→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   379→
   380→**Given** Key Vault diagnostic settings are configured
   381→**When** any pod accesses a Key Vault secret
   382→**Then** the access event is recorded with caller identity (managed identity), timestamp, and operation type
   383→**And** audit logs are retained for minimum 90 days
   384→**And** monthly completeness verification shows < 1% discrepancy between access count and application request count
   385→
   386→---
   387→
   388→## Epic 4: Automated CI/CD & Code Governance
   389→
   390→Marcos submits code via PR, CI validates automatically, Christian reviews and approves, and ArgoCD deploys via GitOps — the complete governance pipeline.
   391→
   392→### Story 4.1: Configure Azure DevOps Repository and PR Workflow
   393→
   394→As a Business Developer (Marcos),
   395→I want to push code to Azure DevOps and create Pull Requests,
   396→So that my changes enter the governance review process.
   397→
   398→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   399→
   400→**Given** Azure DevOps project and repository are configured
   401→**When** a developer pushes code and creates a PR
   402→**Then** the PR is visible to reviewers with diff view
   403→**And** reviewers can comment on specific code lines requesting changes
   404→**And** branch policies require at least one approval before merge
   405→**And** the repository structure follows the monorepo convention (`k8s/apps/<namespace>/<cronjob>/`)
   406→
   407→### Story 4.2: Build CI Validation Pipeline
   408→
   409→As a Code Reviewer (Christian),
   410→I want the CI pipeline to automatically validate builds, scan for secrets, check CVEs, and verify template compliance,
   411→So that I review only code that passes automated quality gates.
   412→
   413→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   414→
   415→**Given** a PR is created or updated
   416→**When** the CI pipeline triggers automatically
   417→**Then** Docker image builds successfully from the developer's Dockerfile
   418→**And** secret scanning (TruffleHog/CredScan) runs and blocks PRs containing detected credentials
   419→**And** Trivy scans the container image with zero Critical/High CVEs allowed
   420→**And** template compliance is verified (src/ structure, structlog config, README completeness)
   421→**And** pipeline results are visible alongside the PR
   422→**And** total pipeline time is < 10 minutes
   423→**And** pipeline trigger reliability is >= 99%
   424→
   425→### Story 4.3: Enable PR Approval and Automated Image Push
   426→
   427→As a Code Reviewer (Christian),
   428→I want approved and merged PRs to automatically build and push Docker images to ACR,
   429→So that approved code progresses to deployment without manual steps.
   430→
   431→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   432→
   433→**Given** a PR passes CI validation and is approved
   434→**When** the reviewer merges the PR to the main branch
   435→**Then** the CI pipeline builds the Docker image
   436→**And** the image is tagged with Git SHA (immutable) and `latest-<env>` (mutable)
   437→**And** the image is pushed to Azure Container Registry
   438→**And** the image is available for ArgoCD to sync
   439→
   440→### Story 4.4: Deploy ArgoCD with ApplicationSets for GitOps
   441→
   442→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   443→I want ArgoCD deployed with ApplicationSets that auto-discover namespaces from the Git repository,
   444→So that deployments are declarative, self-healing, and scale automatically.
   445→
   446→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   447→
   448→**Given** ArgoCD is installed on the AKS cluster
   449→**When** a new namespace directory appears in `k8s/apps/<namespace>/`
   450→**Then** ArgoCD automatically creates an Application for that namespace
   451→**And** ArgoCD syncs CronJob manifests from Git to the cluster within 5 minutes of merge
   452→**And** if a CronJob manifest is manually modified in-cluster, ArgoCD reverts to Git state within 3 minutes (NFR12)
   453→**And** manual approval gate exists for DEV to PRD promotion
   454→**And** ArgoCD watches configured repository folders per namespace
   455→
   456→---
   457→
   458→## Epic 5: Monitoring, Logging & Observability
   459→
   460→Ana can see execution status and logs from the dashboard without asking IT. Juliano has full platform-wide visibility into all namespaces, execution history, and resource consumption.
   461→
   462→### Story 5.1: Deploy LGTM Observability Stack
   463→
   464→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   465→I want Robusta (with embedded Prometheus + Grafana), Mimir, Loki, and Tempo deployed with Azure Storage backends,
   466→So that the monitoring infrastructure can collect metrics, logs, and traces.
   467→
   468→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   469→
   470→**Given** AKS cluster is operational and Azure Storage Account with 3 containers exists
   471→**When** Terraform monitoring module is applied
   472→**Then** Robusta is deployed with kube-prometheus-stack (Prometheus + Grafana + AlertManager)
   473→**And** Prometheus remote-writes metrics to Mimir (Azure Blob: `mimir-blocks`)
   474→**And** Loki is deployed collecting logs to Azure Blob (`loki-chunks`)
   475→**And** Tempo is deployed collecting traces to Azure Blob (`tempo-traces`)
   476→**And** Single Grafana instance connects to Mimir, Loki, and Tempo as data sources
   477→**And** Prometheus scraping interval is <= 30 seconds for CronJob metrics
   478→**And** OTel Operator is deployed with Python auto-instrumentation CR per namespace
   479→
   480→### Story 5.2: Create Platform and Namespace Dashboards
   481→
   482→As an Operations Manager (Ana),
   483→I want Grafana dashboards showing platform overview and per-namespace drill-downs,
   484→So that I can monitor all scripts without asking developers.
   485→
   486→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   487→
   488→**Given** LGTM stack is operational with metrics flowing
   489→**When** Ana opens the Grafana dashboard
   490→**Then** Platform Overview dashboard shows all namespaces, total jobs, and aggregate success rates
   491→**And** Namespace View dashboard shows per-area drill-down with job status and resource usage
   492→**And** dashboard page loads in < 3 seconds for up to 30 days of history
   493→**And** all dashboard access requires corporate AD (SSO) authentication
   494→
   495→### Story 5.3: Enable Execution History and Log Viewing
   496→
   497→As an Operations Manager (Ana),
   498→I want to view execution history, duration trends, and drill into logs for each CronJob,
   499→So that I can troubleshoot issues independently without asking developers.
   500→
   501→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   502→
   503→**Given** CronJobs are running and producing metrics and logs
   504→**When** Ana navigates to a CronJob detail dashboard
   505→**Then** execution history shows minimum last 100 runs or 90 days with duration and status per run
   506→**And** duration trend visualization shows performance over time
   507→**And** clicking a specific execution links to its logs in Loki
   508→**And** log query response time is < 5 seconds for last 7 days of logs
   509→
   510→### Story 5.4: Collect and Display Resource Consumption Metrics
   511→
   512→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   513→I want per-CronJob CPU, memory, and duration metrics collected and displayed,
   514→So that I can right-size resource quotas and identify resource-hungry scripts.
   515→
   516→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   517→
   518→**Given** Prometheus is scraping CronJob pod metrics
   519→**When** Juliano views the resource metrics dashboard
   520→**Then** CPU time consumed (request vs actual) is displayed per CronJob
   521→**And** memory consumed (request vs actual) is displayed per CronJob
   522→**And** execution duration is displayed per CronJob
   523→**And** Loki collects logs centrally across all namespaces for all job executions
   524→
   525→---
   526→
   527→## Epic 6: Cost Tracking & Chargeback
   528→
   529→Ana and Juliano can track compute costs per script and per business area, and export monthly chargeback reports for finance.
   530→
   531→### Story 6.1: Implement Per-CronJob Cost Tracking
   532→
   533→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   534→I want compute costs (CPU-seconds, memory-seconds) calculated per CronJob execution,
   535→So that costs are attributable to individual scripts.
   536→
   537→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   538→
   539→**Given** resource consumption metrics are collected per CronJob
   540→**When** a CronJob execution completes
   541→**Then** CPU-seconds consumed is calculated from Prometheus/Mimir metrics
   542→**And** memory-seconds consumed is calculated from Prometheus/Mimir metrics
   543→**And** execution duration is recorded
   544→**And** cost data is queryable via Grafana
   545→
   546→### Story 6.2: Aggregate and Display Namespace Cost Dashboards
   547→
   548→As an Operations Manager (Ana),
   549→I want cost breakdowns per CronJob and per namespace with daily/weekly/monthly aggregation,
   550→So that I can review spending patterns and identify cost outliers.
   551→
   552→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   553→
   554→**Given** per-CronJob cost data is available in Mimir
   555→**When** Ana opens the Cost Tracking dashboard
   556→**Then** individual CronJob costs are displayed with trends
   557→**And** namespace-level cost aggregation is available (daily, weekly, monthly)
   558→**And** cost breakdown per CronJob within a namespace is visible
   559→
   560→### Story 6.3: Export Monthly Chargeback Reports
   561→
   562→As an Operations Manager (Ana),
   563→I want to export monthly cost reports per namespace in CSV format,
   564→So that I can submit chargeback data to finance.
   565→
   566→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   567→
   568→**Given** monthly cost data is aggregated per namespace
   569→**When** Ana uses Grafana dashboard export
   570→**Then** CSV file is generated with per-CronJob cost breakdown for the selected month
   571→**And** namespace total is included
   572→**And** the export includes columns: CronJob name, execution count, total CPU-seconds, total memory-seconds, total duration, estimated cost
   573→
   574→---
   575→
   576→## Epic 7: Alerting & Incident Response
   577→
   578→Ana and Juliano receive proactive notifications via Teams and Email when jobs fail or behave anomalously, with severity-based routing and full alert history.
   579→
   580→### Story 7.1: Configure Alert Routing to Teams and Email
   581→
   582→As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
   583→I want Robusta configured to send enriched alerts to Teams and Email based on severity levels,
   584→So that the right people are notified of issues at the right urgency.
   585→
   586→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   587→
   588→**Given** Robusta is deployed with playbooks and sinks configured
   589→**When** a job fails after all 3 retries
   590→**Then** an enriched alert (with pod logs, resource usage, suggested fix) is sent to Teams
   591→**And** Critical severity alerts go to Teams OPS channel + Email to on-call (immediate response)
   592→**And** High severity alerts go to Teams OPS channel + Email (business hours response)
   593→**And** Medium severity alerts go to Email only (next business day review)
   594→**And** Low severity alerts appear in Grafana dashboard only (weekly review)
   595→**And** alert is delivered within 60 seconds of the condition trigger
   596→**And** alert is sent within 2 minutes of final retry failure
   597→**And** mean failure detection time is < 5 minutes across all alert types (NFR10)
   598→
   599→### Story 7.2: Implement Anomaly Detection Alerts
   600→
   601→As an Operations Manager (Ana),
   602→I want alerts when a job's execution time exceeds 2x its historical average,
   603→So that performance degradation is caught before it impacts business processes.
   604→
   605→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   606→
   607→**Given** historical execution duration data exists for a CronJob
   608→**When** a job execution time exceeds 2x the rolling average
   609→**Then** a medium-severity alert is generated
   610→**And** the alert includes the current duration, historical average, and the deviation factor
   611→**And** the alert is routed according to severity rules (medium to Email)
   612→
   613→### Story 7.3: Create Alert Dashboard and History
   614→
   615→As an Operations Manager (Ana),
   616→I want to view active alerts and full alert history in Grafana,
   617→So that I have a complete record of incidents for governance reporting.
   618→
   619→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   620→
   621→**Given** alerts are being generated and routed
   622→**When** Ana opens the Alerts dashboard
   623→**Then** active alerts are displayed with severity, source CronJob, namespace, and timestamp
   624→**And** alert history is searchable and filterable by date range, severity, and namespace
   625→**And** alert severity distribution is visualized
   626→
